topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the quiet finality of a closing door. Subject line: Retirement Confirmation. No pleasantries. No mention of the projects we’d been tackling, no simple 'goodbye.' Just a formal notification that Dr. Alistair Finch was officially off the clock. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening almost imperceptibly. I had spent the last six months under his tutelage, pouring my entire energy into mastering the complex algorithms he’d taught me. Now, it was just… gone. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning under my weight. I picked up my lukewarm coffee, the ceramic feeling suddenly heavy and pointless in my hand. I wanted to ask him, just once, what the next phase looked like, but the words caught in my throat, stuck behind a sudden, sharp irritation.

[story 2]

I found out via a colleague, a roundabout, unsatisfying route. When I finally saw Alistair at the office reception, he was there, looking remarkably composed, almost serene. He offered a brief, practiced smile, the kind reserved for public appearances. "It's been time," he said, his tone even, utterly devoid of the warmth I’d grown accustomed to. It was too neat. Too sudden. I felt a distinct clenching in my chest, a tension that made my shoulders rise toward my ears. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. "Right," I managed, my voice sounding unnecessarily clipped. I nodded once, a stiff, minimal gesture, and turned to leave before I could formulate a proper response, the silence between us thick with unspoken expectation and my own simmering dissatisfaction.

[story 3]

The notice was tacked to the departmental bulletin board, a stark piece of paper against the faded beige. Alistair had stepped down. No farewell note, no invitation for a final coffee. I walked over, my footsteps measured and deliberate on the linoleum floor. I picked up the paper, my fingers tracing the printed words. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening began in my neck muscles. It wasn't anger, not exactly; it was a deep-seated feeling of being abruptly dismissed, of having my trajectory suddenly rerouted without consultation. I crumpled the paper slightly in my fist, the sound a soft, dry rustle. I dropped it back onto the board, straightening up, forcing my expression into a neutral mask. I needed to find something else to focus on, anything to occupy the space where that abrupt absence had left a noticeable void.
